name: Antelope
short_description: BLM rockhounding site near Antelope offering agate and jasper collecting with 25 lbs daily limit. Gentle slopes and easy access make this beginner-friendly.
description:
Antelope sits on public BLM land in Wasco County's high desert, a prime rockhounding destination for collectors seeking Oregon's characteristic agates and jaspers. This location features gentle terrain at 2,663 feet elevation, making it accessible for rockhounds of all skill levels. The rolling landscape includes multiple collecting areas where chalcedony and obsidian specimens can be found among the basalt-derived deposits.

The geology here reflects the Owyhee-Malheur uplands province, where Miocene and Pliocene basaltic volcanism deposited silica-rich materials that weathered into nodular agate and jasper formations. Ground-level searching near exposed hillsides and in shallow washes yields the best specimens. Unlike fee dig sites, this public BLM land requires no permit and allows rockhounders to explore freely within the 25-pound daily personal-use limit.

The Antelope area is less crowded than famous Oregon agate beds further east, offering quieter collecting opportunities. Visitors should come prepared for high desert conditions with variable weather and bring adequate water. The site works well for families and beginners due to gentle slopes and straightforward access.
history: This region sits within the high desert province where extensive Miocene-age basaltic flows and subsequent silica-rich hot spring activity deposited the agate and jasper nodules found throughout Wasco County. Rockhounding activity here expanded significantly in the mid-20th century as Oregon's gemstone reputation grew, making sites like Antelope popular among recreational collectors seeking quality specimens without traveling to remote locations.
tips: Search the shallow washes and exposed hillside cuts where erosion naturally concentrates nodules - look for rounded, translucent stones with banding patterns typical of Oregon agates. Bring a 5-gallon bucket and screening tool to process fine material along the wash bottoms where smaller chalcedony pieces accumulate. Early morning visits yield better results as the sun angle highlights glossy stone surfaces against the tan-colored soil.
terrain_notes: Elevation 2,663 feet with gentle rolling slopes and sparse high desert vegetation including sage and juniper. Terrain is firm and walkable with occasional loose scree and weathered basalt fragments. Parking pullouts are available near access roads with easy walking distance (typically under 0.5 miles) to productive collecting areas. Footing is stable on slope faces but watch for loose rock on steep banks.
best_season: April - October. High desert weather is mild during spring and early fall with comfortable daytime temperatures. Summer heat (July-August) can be intense with temperatures reaching 85-90 degrees F, making early morning or late afternoon collecting preferable. Winter (November-March) brings occasional snow and freezing conditions that can make high elevation terrain treacherous.
written_directions: From Antelope, head east on local roads toward the BLM access points. The nearest public land parcels are accessed via county roads near the town; consult BLM maps for specific pullouts and parking areas. GPS navigation is recommended to locate exact parking coordinates. Roads are typically rough dirt with moderate clearance requirements; check locally for current road conditions as high desert washes can become impassable after heavy rain.
road_conditions: Rough dirt roads with light washboard; high-clearance passenger vehicles recommended. Some parking areas accessible via improved county roads.
parking_notes: Informal pullouts and parking areas near BLM access points. No developed lot facilities; park off-road as space allows.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation. Remove only loose specimens and surface material.
rules:
Personal use only
Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation
No specimen claim staking
Stay on or near public land boundaries
nearest_services: Antelope (town) approximately 0-2 miles for limited services; Madras approximately 20 miles for full services including gas, food, and water.
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat to gently rolling terrain accessible to most rockhounders. Unpaved dirt roads to parking areas; passenger vehicles with moderate clearance recommended. Walking distances to productive areas typically under 0.5 miles with stable footing. Some exposed hillside work requires stepping over loose basalt fragments.

faq.question: What types of agates and stones can I find rockhounding near Antelope, Oregon?
faq.answer: The Antelope BLM area yields high-quality Oregon agates, jasper, chalcedony nodules, and obsidian fragments. Most specimens are found as nodules with translucent to opaque banding in earth tones - reds, yellows, blacks, and grays. Chalcedony is abundant in shallow washes where it weathers from larger formations.

faq.question: Do I need a permit to rockhound at Antelope on BLM land?
faq.answer: No permit is required for recreational rockhounding on BLM land near Antelope. This is public land open for personal use collecting under FLPMA guidelines. You do not need written permission, but you must follow the 25-pound daily limit and collect only for personal use, not commercial purposes.

faq.question: How much can I collect per day rockhounding near Antelope, Oregon?
faq.answer: The BLM allows a maximum of 25 pounds per day per person for personal use collection. This weight limit applies to all specimens you remove from the site. Commercial collection and resale are not permitted on this public land.

faq.question: What is the best season to go rockhounding at Antelope, Wasco County?
faq.answer: April through October is ideal, when high desert temperatures are mild and roads are passable. Avoid summer peak heat (July-August) by collecting early morning or late afternoon. Winter snow and freezing conditions (November-March) can make access difficult and dangerous.

faq.question: Is Antelope rockhounding site suitable for beginners and families with children?
faq.answer: Yes, Antelope is highly beginner-friendly. Terrain is relatively flat with gentle slopes, easy parking access, and productive surface collecting without advanced skills needed. Children ages 6 and up can participate safely. The site offers good results for casual collectors without requiring specialized equipment or technical knowledge.

faq.question: What tools and supplies should I bring for rockhounding at Antelope near Madras?
faq.answer: Bring a 5-gallon bucket for transport, small hand tools (chisel and hammer for loose specimens), screening mesh for fine material in washes, sunscreen, hat, and 2+ liters of water per person. Wear sturdy boots due to sharp basalt fragments. A field guide to Oregon agates helps identify specimens. GPS or printed BLM maps ensure you stay on public land.

meta_title: Antelope Rockhounding - Oregon Agate BLM Site
meta_description: Free BLM rockhounding near Antelope, Oregon. Collect agate, jasper, and chalcedony. No permit needed. 25 lbs daily limit. Beginner-friendly.
